rulings.Case Title: CROWDSTRIKE, INC. VS. DFND SECURITY, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Petitioner And Crossrespondent Crowdstrike, Inc. To Lift Stay Of Proceedings
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Tuesday, April 29, 2025, Line 1. PETITIONER CROWDSTRIKE, INC.'s Motion Of Petitioner And Crossrespondent Crowdstrike, Inc. To Lift Stay Of Proceedings. The unopposed motion to lift the stay of proceedings is granted. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: TIEMING YAO VS. YANYING LIU ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT / Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Tuesday, April 29, 2025, Line 2. PLAINTIFF TIEMING YAO's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Plaintiff Tieming Yao's motion for summary judgment is denied. A summary judgment motion "shall be heard no later than 30 days before the date of trial, unless the court for good cause orders otherwise." (CCP 437c(a)(3).) Trial in this matter is set for May 5, 2025. Yao offers no basis to support a good cause finding. Independently, even were the court to reach the merits, the court concludes that a triable issue of fact exists on whether defendant Yanying Liu transferred his interests in the sale of the 745 Victoria Street property "[w]ith actual intent to hinder, delay, or defraud any creditor of the debtor." (CCP 3439.04(a)(1).) "Whether a debtor had the actual intent to hinder, delay, or defraud a creditor is a question of fact." (Aghaian v. Minassian (2020) 59 Cal.App.5th 447, 456.) While plaintiff Yao points to the presence of a number of indicia of bad intent, Liu and co-defendant Qingling Xu present evidence that the transfer was instead motivated by a desire to keep the family assets in the United States where the couple's children resided when the marriage resolved. (Defendants' Declaration on the Status of Two Defendants' '745 Property,' pp. 4-8.) Because there is a triable issue of fact on the second cause of action for violation of the uniform voidable transactions act, and Yao has moved only for summary judgment on the entire case and not summary adjudication on individual causes of action, the court need not consider whether there is a triable issue of fact on the first cause of action for recognition of the foreign judgment. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. rulings.Case Title: DANIELLE DI BARI VS. WOODLINE PARTNERS LP, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For A Protective Order For The Deposition Of Dr Anthony Reading To Occur Remotely Declaration Of Alex Dibona Esq Exhibits
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Tuesday, April 29, 2025, Line 6. PLAINTIFF DANIELLE KESICH's Motion For A Protective Order For The Deposition Of Dr Anthony Reading To Occur Remotely. Plaintiff Danielle Di Bari's motion for a protective order allowing her retained expert Dr. Anthony Reading to be deposed remotely is denied. CCP 2034.420 states, "The deposition of any expert described in subdivision (b) of Section 2034.210 shall be taken at a place that is within 75 miles of the courthouse where the action is pending. On motion for a protective order by the party designating an expert witness, and on a showing of exceptional hardship, the court may order that the deposition be taken at a more distant place from the courthouse." Di Bari does not make a specific showing of "exceptional hardship." Her showing is based on (1) defendants' initial acquiescence to a remote deposition; and (2) the assertion that Reading continues to be affected by the Los Angeles wildfires and the loss of his home. The court is certainly sympathetic, but given the passage of time and the lack of specific showing how Reading would be affected by having to travel, finds that the latter reason does not suffice as a showing of exceptional hardship. The former assertion is not relevant, as the deposition where defendants agreed to depose Reading remotely did not occur. Defendants are not required to make a showing of prejudice from a remote deposition; rather, the burden is on Di Bari to show reason to excuse her expert's compliance with 2034.420. Defendants seek sanctions. CCP 2025.420(h) provides that "The court shall impose a monetary sanction under Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 2023.010) against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion for a protective order, unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." The court does not find Di Bari acted with substantial justification; her position on this motion was not supported by authorities. The court awards $1000 in sanctions payable by Di Bari to defendants payable within 30 days of this order. Defendants seek a much higher amount but the court finds the amount of time Defendants spent on this straightforward motion to be unreasonable and does not award the full amount. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. rulings.Case Title: ALFREDO GASPAR GONZALEZ VS. GENERAL MOTORS, LLC ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Tuesday, April 29, 2025, Line 3. 1 - DEFENDANT GENERAL MOTORS, LLC's MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST Amended COMPLAINT. Defendant General Motor's LLC motion to strike the prayer for punitive damages from plaintiff Alfredo Gaspar Gonzalez's First Amended Complaint (FAC) is denied. By companion order, the court overrules the demurrer to Gaspar Gonzalez's fraudulent concealment claim. This claim can support punitive damages. (Civ. Code 3294.) The fraudulent concealment alleged in the FAC-that the transmission defect that was knowingly concealed presented a safety hazard (FAC paras. 28, 68) because the defect "can suddenly and unexpectedly cause the driver to be unable to control the speed and acceleration/deceleration of the vehicle," and GM knew of the defect but concealed it (FAC para. 68)-is sufficient to support a punitive damages award if proven. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. rulings.Case Title: ELDA GONZALEZ VS. FORD MOTOR COMPANY, A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff´S Request For Production Of Documents, Set One
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Tuesday, April 29, 2025, Line 7 (PART 1 OF 2 OF THE TENTATIVE RULING). PLAINTIFF ELDA GONZALEZ's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff's Request For Production Of Documents, Set One. Plaintiff Elda Gonzalez's motion to compel further responses to her request for production of documents, set one, from Ford Motor Company is continued on the court's own motion to July 16, 2025. The court finds that Gonzalez did not sufficiently meet and confer before bringing this motion. Gonzalez sent a meet and confer letter to Ford that attached two sample discovery orders. On January 15, 2025, Ford offered to produce the documents outlined in those sample orders, subjective to a protective order, and offered a time to confer telephonically. (Tully Dec. para. 6 & Ex. 3.) Gonzalez did not respond to Ford's letter, other than to sign the proposed protective order that was enclosed, and instead moved to compel without any further efforts to narrow the dispute. The court orders Gonzalez to meet and confer telephonically or in person concerning the compromise Ford offered in its letter of January 15. The court is hopeful that the parties will resolve their outstanding discovery disputes. The motion is continued to July 16 unless it is withdrawn by Gonzalez in the interim. Gonzalez shall file a supplemental brief no later than nine court days in advance of the continued hearing date concerning what issues are still in dispute. She must also file an updated separate statement that shows specific facts and good cause to order production of any documents that remain in dispute. Ford may respond by supplemental brief and updated separate statement by no later than five court days in advance of the hearing. CCP 2031.300(c) provides that "the court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel a response to a demand for inspection . . . unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." The failure to meet and confer is a misuse of the discovery process and burdens the court with reviewing disputes that the parties could resolve through good-faith efforts. (CCP 2023.010(i).) The court finds that Gonzalez acted without substantial justification in failing to meet and confer and orders her to pay sanctions of $500 to the San Francisco Superior Court within 10 court days of entry of this order. (END OF PART 1 of 2 OF THE TENTATIVE RULING). =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: THERESA BAYLISS-PEDROTTI VS. CALTRANS ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Tuesday, April 29, 2025, Line 8 (PART 1 OF 2 OF THE TENTATIVE RULING). DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA HIGHWAY PATROL's DEMURRER to COMPLAINT. Defendant California Highway Patrol (CHP) demurs to count one of Theresa Bayliss-Pedrotti's complaint alleging a cause of action against CHP for dangerous condition of public property on the Bay Bridge. The case arises out of an accident. According to the complaint, an intoxicated person ran out of gas on the bridge and stopped in a traffic lane, and Bayliss-Pedrotti's daughter's car ran into the intoxicated person's stopped car. While the daughter was stopped to exchange information with the intoxicated person, a third car struck her, and the accident was fatal. CHP (and Caltrans, which has answered the complaint) are alleged to have maintained a dangerous condition of public property because they own or control the stretch of I-80 where the accidents occurred, and because they undertook to monitor the stretch and keep it clear of accidents. CHP contends first that it does not own or control the stretch of I-80 where the accidents occurred, relying on a case that distinguished CHP's and Caltrans's statutory roles. (See Greyhound Lines v Department of California Highway Patrol (2013) 213 Cal. App. 4th, 1129.) That is ordinarily true, but Bayliss-Pedrotti offers to plead facts that CHP partners with Caltrans to jointly manage and control traffic operations on the Bay Bridge and to oversee management of collisions like this one. The court grants Bayliss-Pedrotti leave to amend. CHP contends that leave would be futile to permit Bayliss-Pedrotti leave to amend because even if CHP owned or controlled the stretch of I-80, she fails to state a claim for dangerous condition of public property in the fact of a blocked lane resulting from an accident. CHP relies on Zelig v. County of Los Angeles (2002) 27 Cal.4th 1112 [failure to prevent criminal acts of third parties is not a dangerous condition of public property]. But Zelig emphasized that "liability is imposed only when there is some defect in the property itself and a causal connection is established between the defect and the injury." (Id. at p. 1135.) Here, Bayliss-Pedrotti alleges a causal connection, e.g. in the lack of "warning signage, turn outs, [or] signage advising what to do in the event of a collision." (Compl. para. 30(a).) CHP's remaining arguments are not susceptible to resolution on demurrer. The court denies Bayliss-Pedrotti's request for judicial notice of the truth of facts stated in a government website but grants her leave to amend to allege facts concerning CHP's undertaking to control the Bay Bridge. (END OF PART 1 OF 2 OF THE TENTATIVE RULING) =(301/CVA)
